skill.name: wp-block-development
skill.description: Develop WordPress Gutenberg blocks with guidance for block.json metadata, registration, attributes, dynamic rendering, deprecations, supports, and testing workflows.
skill.summary: Guides safe, modern WordPress Gutenberg block development from scaffolding through validation.
skill.author: Automattic

security_audit.summary: AI review dismissed the high static score as mostly false positives from Markdown inline code, WordPress documentation URLs, and terms such as apiVersion rather than executable shell, network, or cryptographic behavior. The only confirmed capability is a local Node.js helper that scans for block.json files and prints block metadata, so the skill is low risk and safe to publish with normal command review.

low_findings.title: Local Filesystem Block Metadata Scan
low_findings.description: The helper script walks the current working directory, reads block.json files, and prints a report that includes the repository root and block metadata. This is legitimate for a WordPress block development skill, but users should run it only inside intended project directories because it enumerates local files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem behavior is direct and confirmed by fs.readdirSync, fs.readFileSync, and process.stdout.write. It is bounded to local scanning and does not transmit data, which keeps severity low.

low_findings.title: User-Visible Development Commands
low_findings.description: The skill instructs the assistant to run local triage, block listing, build, lint, and test commands during WordPress block work. These are normal development actions, but project scripts can have side effects and should be reviewed before execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are plainly documented and relevant to the skill purpose. No hidden command execution or user-input command construction was found, so this is a low operational caution.

low_findings.title: Documentation Links and Package References
low_findings.description: Static network findings are documentation URLs and package references for official WordPress resources and npm tooling. They do not represent code that performs network requests, although scaffolding packages may require network access when a user chooses to run them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are Markdown links and package names, not fetch calls or HTTP client code. The context supports documentation and tooling discovery rather than data exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dismissed Static Pattern: Markdown Inline Code
dangerous_patterns.description: The many reported Ruby backtick and weak cryptography hits are false positives caused by Markdown inline code, WordPress field names, apiVersion text, and documentation examples. No Ruby execution, cryptographic operation, prompt injection, or suspicious obfuscation was found in the reviewed files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Review of the cited lines shows prose and inline Markdown code rather than executable code paths. The static heuristic matched formatting tokens and WordPress terms, not dangerous behavior.

content.user_title: Build WordPress Blocks with Safer Workflows
content.value_statement: WordPress block development can break saved content when metadata, attributes, or markup change without a plan. This skill guides block.json updates, registration, rendering, migrations, and verification steps.
actual_capabilities: Helps locate block roots and inspect block.json metadata in a repository.
Guides creation of new blocks with official WordPress scaffolding workflows.
Explains safe changes to attributes, saved markup, supports, and wrapper attributes.
Supports dynamic block planning with render files and PHP registration patterns.
Provides debugging paths for invalid blocks, missing assets, and apiVersion warnings.
Recommends build, lint, E2E, and manual verification checks for block changes.
limitations: Does not replace testing in a real WordPress environment.
May suggest npm, WP-CLI, or project scripts that need user review before execution.
Does not guarantee compatibility with every WordPress or Gutenberg release.
Provides guidance, but final PHP and JavaScript security review remains required.

use_cases.title: Create a new block plugin
use_cases.description: Plan a new Gutenberg block, choose static or dynamic rendering, scaffold the project, and verify the initial build.

use_cases.title: Repair invalid block content
use_cases.description: Diagnose serialization problems, attribute parsing issues, and saved markup changes that cause invalid block warnings.

use_cases.title: Modernize existing block metadata
use_cases.description: Update block.json fields, apiVersion, asset declarations, supports, and migrations while preserving existing content.

prompt_templates.title: Locate Existing Blocks
prompt_templates.prompt: Find the WordPress blocks in this repository. Summarize each block root, block.json metadata, and any immediate compatibility concerns.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a New Block
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a new Gutenberg block for this plugin. Recommend static or dynamic rendering, required files, and verification steps.

prompt_templates.title: Fix Attribute Persistence
prompt_templates.prompt: This block does not save its attributes correctly. Review the attribute definitions, saved markup, selectors, and migration needs.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Migration Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to change saved markup for an existing block. Design a deprecation and migration plan that protects old posts.

output_examples.input: Review an existing block that fails after reload.
output: A diagnosis of likely serialization or attribute selector mismatch.
A migration checklist for older saved content.
A short verification plan for editor reload and frontend output.
A recommendation for render file placement and PHP registration.
Asset loading notes for editor and frontend behavior.
Manual and automated checks for output parity.

output_examples.input: Modernize block metadata for a current WordPress version.
output: A prioritized list of block.json changes.
Compatibility notes for apiVersion, assets, and iframe editor behavior.
Risks that require tests before release.

best_practices: Run the repository's existing lint, build, and test commands after block changes.
Add deprecations before changing saved markup or attribute shapes.
Review suggested npm, WP-CLI, and project scripts before running them.
anti_patterns: Renaming a block without a compatibility plan for existing content.
Changing saved markup without fixtures or deprecated versions.
Adding frontend assets to editor-only fields or editor assets to frontend fields.

faq.question: Does this skill create complete WordPress plugins?
faq.answer: It guides block plugin structure and scaffolding, but generated code still needs project-specific review and testing.

faq.question: Can it fix invalid block warnings?
faq.answer: It can help diagnose common causes and design migrations, but old post fixtures are needed for reliable verification.

faq.question: Does it run commands automatically?
faq.answer: The skill recommends commands for triage and testing. Users should approve and inspect commands before execution.

faq.question: Does it require network access?
faq.answer: The guidance links to WordPress documentation and may suggest package scaffolding. The included local script does not make network requests.

faq.question: Which tools does it support?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Is it only for dynamic blocks?
faq.answer: No. It covers static blocks, dynamic blocks, inner blocks, metadata, attributes, deprecations, and verification workflows.
